items.title: [ScienceDaily] Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Box Hill War Memorial in Box Hill, Australia
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

The sight and sound of the bugle, a simple brass instrument, is one that many associate with remembrance. This memorial, in the middle of Box Hill Park, acts as a poignant symbol to remember local lives lost in World War I. 

The monument was erected in 1922 and unveiled by the local Governor-General Lord Forster. The names of those who lost their lives during the war are commemorated on a plaque, all listed individually. The marble soldier stands holding a bugle to his lips and sits on a granite column. It was erected by the Council of the Shire of Nunawading. 

A wall has been erected in the area surrounding the memorial, and on this wall now lie several plaques that pay tribute to lives lost in other wars in which Australian citizens have served. 

There is also another sculpture at the site that is equally as poignant and pays tribute to lives lost in World War II. This depicts a soldier carrying a wounded comrade on his back and has the word 'Kokoda Trail' written underneath. This symbolizes the Kokoda Trail campaign, part of the Pacific War of WWII, which consisted of several battles fought from July to November 1942 in the then Australian Territory of Papua.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The House of David Bowie in Berlin, Germany
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

The house is nothing special, and the neighborhood even less so. Flanked by a tattoo parlor and a physiotherapy clinic, the entrance to Hauptstraße 155 is an archway made of cast cement stone—a common feature of the Gründerzeit (Wilhelminian era)—featuring a massive oak door decorated with cast-iron floral ornaments. The door lock has two keyholes set at a 90-degree angle to each other, designed for the "pass-through" keys of the early 20th century, which were intended to force the residents of these tenement blocks to lock up. The beautiful door is defaced with several hastily sprayed tags, the sidewalk is dirty, and the surrounding area is shaped by a high proportion of immigrants.

 

Real roses have been tucked into the metal flowers of the door; now, in late February, they are already withered. They are dedicated to the house's prominent former resident and were placed in his memory either on January 8th (his birthday) or January 10th (the anniversary of his death).

 

David Bowie lived in a seven-room apartment on the first floor of this house from 1976 to 1978. This period was marked, on one hand, by a turning away from the drugs that had previously dominated his life in Los Angeles and, on the other, by extreme creativity; the iconic albums Low, “Heroes”, and Lodger—the so-called "Berlin Trilogy"—were created here. In 1976, Bowie was a wreck: emaciated, surviving only on milk, cocaine, and cigarettes. Depression came along, and the marriage with Angie wouldn't work.

 

The strange, isolated location of the divided city, his fascination with the Weimar Republic, Bertolt Brecht, and the Brücke painters, as well as the creative input from avant-garde bands like Can, Kraftwerk, and Neu!, were the deciding factors in his move to Berlin.

 

After the landlady, Rosa Morath, evicted a lawyer involved in the APO (student protest movement) who had painted all seven rooms black and wanted to start a commune, an agent reached out to rent the apartment for a British artist. The condition was that he be allowed to paint the apartment white at his own expense. Permission was granted, and thus David Bowie became a Berliner. He also brought along Iggy Pop, who initially lived with him but was eventually evicted from the main apartment and banished to the rear building for raiding the fridge (specifically, expensive delicacies from KaDeWe). This did not harm their friendship or creative partnership; in 1977, Bowie composed and produced what are arguably the best albums of Iggy Pop’s career: The Idiot and Lust for Life. Contemporary witnesses report that Bowie’s apartment was furnished rather sparsely, while Iggy’s place was appropriately "shabby."

 

In photos from this period, Bowie usually looks unremarkable, which allowed him to evade public attention in Berlin; furthermore, his fame continued to grow later due to the music composed here. He is said to have often cycled to Hansa Studios, with a view from the window directly onto the Berlin Wall. The sight of a couple kissing in front of the VoPos (border guards) inspired him to write the song “Heroes”—the ultimate West Berlin anthem.

 

Other Berlin haunts for him included the Dschungel, Schwarzes Café, Chez Romy Haag, and, just a few steps from his front door, the gay bar Anderes Ufer, which still exists today as Neues Ufer.

 

To the right of the house entrance is a lovely piece of graffiti from his Ziggy Stardust / Aladdin Sane phase, and above it, a Berlin commemorative plaque has been mounted since August 2016.

 

There were considerations to rename this part of Hauptstraße to David-Bowie-Straße, but so far, these plans have faltered due to the slow-moving wheels of Berlin's bureaucracy.

 

 

 

We can be heroes, just for one day...

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and then by Euro-American trappers and settlers into the 19th century. It was also an obvious route for a railroad, and the Northern Pacific Railway was built through it in the early 1880s, along the west bank of the river. (The railroad is still in active use and is now part of the BNSF system.)

Railroading was an extremely labor-intensive business well into the 20th century, and camps were built along the right-of-way to house the trackwalkers, signal men, section gangs, and other workers. In 1885 one such camp was built here at Umtanum Creek, where the eponymous creek joins the Yakima. The camp even boasted a school and a post office by the early 20th century.

When in 1925 the automobile highway was built through the canyon on the east shore of the river, cable suspension bridges were built across the river from the railroad camps to provide highway access. Evidently the original bridges were wide enough to be passable to Ford Model As. In the 1960s, the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife and the Bureau of Land Management took over the day-to-day management of the bridge, but only in 1987 was the bridge deck narrowed to make it accessible to pedestrians only.

With the continuing rise of automotive travel, it proved no longer necessary to station crews on the railroad right-of-way, and by the 1970s the last of the camps had been removed.

The cable suspension bridges were also removed, with the sole exception of the Umtanum Creek bridge. It was still used by recreational users to access the west side of the river, but because of age-related deterioration concerns were being raised about its safety. Meanwhile, efforts were underway to have the bridge declared a national historic site. Finally, Umtanum Creek Bridge was restored in early 2021, with attention paid to preserving the structure's historic values.

items.title: [MacRumors] Four Apple Stores in the U.S. Are Moving This Month
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Four of Apple's retail stores in the U.S. are moving this month, although all of them will only be a short distance from their existing locations.


Apple Queens Center in Elmhurst, New York already moved to a temporary location in the shopping mall on Friday, July 17 while the existing store is remodeled. Apple did not indicate when the existing store will reopen.

Apple Renaissance at Colony Park in Ridgeland, Mississippi is moving to a new spot within the same shopping center on Friday, July 24 at 10 a.m. local time. Apple's new storefront will be located right in front of the Show Fountain.

Apple Briarwood in Ann Arbor, Michigan will be relocating to a new space at the mall on Friday, July 31 at 10 a.m. local time. The entrance to the new, larger store will be in front of The Lawn, a new outdoor space at the mall.

Also on July 31 at 10 a.m. local time, Apple The Promenade Shops at Briargate in Colorado Springs, Colorado is moving six doors down to the left.
Tag: Apple Store

This article, "Four Apple Stores in the U.S. Are Moving This Month" first appeared on MacRumors.com

For the 18th year in a row, you can watch the original CBS News coverage of Walter Cronkite reporting on the Moon landing and the first Moon walk on a small B&W television, synced to the present-day time. Just open this page in your browser today, July 20th, and the coverage will start playing at the proper time. Here’s the schedule (all times EDT):

4:10:30 pm: Moon landing broadcast starts
4:17:40 pm: Lunar module lands on the Moon

4:20:15 pm - 10:51:26 pm: Break in coverage

10:51:27 pm: Moon walk broadcast starts
10:56:15 pm: First step on Moon
11:51:30 pm: Nixon speaks to the Eagle crew
12:00:30 am: Broadcast end (on July 21)

Set an alarm on your phone or calendar! Also, this works best on an actual computer but I think it functions ok on phones and tablets if necessary.

Back in 2018, I wrote a bit about what to look out for when you’re watching the landing:

The radio voices you hear are mostly Mission Control in Houston (specifically Apollo astronaut Charlie Duke, who acted as the spacecraft communicator for this mission) and Buzz Aldrin, whose job during the landing was to keep an eye on the LM’s altitude and speed — you can hear him calling it out, “3 1/2 down, 220 feet, 13 forward.” Armstrong doesn’t say a whole lot…he’s busy flying and furiously searching for a suitable landing site. But it’s Armstrong that says after they land, “Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed.”. Note the change in call sign from “Eagle” to “Tranquility Base”. :)

Two things to listen for on the broadcast: the 1201/1202 program alarms I mentioned above and two quick callouts by Charlie Duke about the remaining fuel towards the end: “60 seconds” and “30 seconds”. Armstrong is taking all this information in through his earpiece — the 1202s, the altitude and speed from Aldrin, and the remaining fuel — and using it to figure out where to land.

items.content_html:

* EU regulators have accused Meta, the company behind Facebook and Instagram, of failing to tackle the risks of its "addictive design" on the physical and mental health of users.*

This is a very important problem, and one of the reasons to refuse to use those systems. But if the EU is serious about protecting users from them, it should take firm action -- take a big bite out of the problem, not a little nibble. Use of a recommendation engine should not be enabled by default.

I think that a platform should not be allowed to use or suggest any particular recommedation engine.

items.title: [MacRumors] EU Drops Battery Removal Requirement for Apple Watch and AirPods
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

The European Commission yesterday adopted new exemptions to its Batteries Regulation that free the Apple Watch and AirPods from having to offer user-removable and replaceable batteries.


The EU's Batteries Regulation generally requires consumer products sold in the region to let users swap out their own batteries, a push meant to keep devices in use longer and make it easier to recover materials for recycling. The Commission has now expanded its list of exempt product types to six additional categories, folding in wearables like smartwatches and fitness trackers.

The reasoning largely mirrors why devices like electric toothbrushes were already exempt: Opening a compact, sealed enclosure and failing to reseal it properly could let water in and create a safety risk. Products can also qualify for exemption if their construction makes battery removal inherently dangerous, or if there is no realistic way to build in user access given current manufacturing methods.

The new categories effectively cover the Apple Watch and AirPods, both of which rely on the kind of small, sealed designs the Commission is now carving out, along with Meta's smart glasses. The change follows months of pressure from U.S. officials over rules that had reportedly complicated ‌Meta‌'s plans to bring its newest display-equipped smart glasses to Europe.

The iPhone was already exempt from the removability rule under the original regulation, thanks to its battery cycle life and water resistance rating. Apple offers battery service through Apple Stores, authorized providers, and its Self Service Repair program, which lets owners handle certain repairs, including batteries, at home. Not every device gets the same pass: Nintendo has now said it will sell a version of the Switch 2 in the EU with a user-replaceable battery to meet the incoming rules.

The delegated act still has to clear scrutiny from the European Parliament and the Council of the EU. It takes effect 20 days after publication in the Official Journal of the EU if neither body objects, ahead of the regulation's wider rollout in 2027.
